Once people perma lock, its over.  Done.  Care or don't care, there is nothing to do.

author: Lysimach | Make Polis Great [STAR]
content: This is exactly the issue I see.  It won't really be a choice.   Once the guy with 10,000 polis permalocks, and now has as much PVP as the guy with 20k polis, the guy with 20k Polis kinda HAS to perma-lock at least 10k, to maintain his advantage.   So he permalocks 20K and now the guy with 50k has to permalock to maintain his advantage.  This snowballs and most people with 5 year locks have permalocks.  Nobody nets any real advantage over the current situation.   Except now they are in forever, with no reason to strategize about Polis locking ever again.   For the new player, his 1 year Polis lock has even less value, making it more discouraging to get involved. As Polis becomes more valued, the disparity between whales and guppies just grows. I envision the day when we have 50,000 players, and Polis is needed by everyone.  As prices climb,  selling some off would benefit everyone.  Trading Polis for other SA assets benefits the economy and provides liquidity to the Polis market.   To take this to a theoretical extreme - Imagine if one dude has 2million Polis permalocked, and Polis really did hit $100? He would wield enormous power, and it would be financially impossible for anyone to challenge him.   Unable to sell his Polis, how else would extract equity?  Exert influence to give himself monopolies?  Sell votes?  Vote to have the DAO pay him out?   Yeah, that is extreme, but it illustrates the risks we can't even imagine right now.  I think Polis should always be available to be traded, re-balance power, and equalize the markets. #SaveCapitalism

author: SCORMUD
content: At the end of the day, I don’t really see the benefit, except maybe anticipating a specific individual dumping their POLIS on the market 5 years later. In any case, the circulating supply of $POLIS is reduced in the same way. And even if that person decides to stop locking their POLIS  5 years, they could still choose to relock them 3 years later, or lock them again for another 5 years if they see an economic incentive to do so. Five years is already a very long time.

author: SCORMUD
content: Of course, you could say that people who commit to the game for life should have a greater stake in it. I agree with that, but there still needs to be some balance somewhere.

author: SCORMUD
content: We could also give greater advantages to a 5-year lock to encourage people to lock for 5 years, and it would probably have a bigger impact in incentivizing long-term locking if the goal is to reduce the circulating supply, because people would still have in the back of their minds that they’ll eventually be able to get their tokens back. On the other hand, a lifetime lock might discourage people from doing it altogether and lead them to lock their tokens for shorter periods instead.

author: SCORMUD
content: Anyway, I don’t think the question is that simple to answer.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
Historically, messages transmitted over the network must not exceed the IPv6 MTU size to ensure a fast and reliable network delivery. Solana has used a conservative MTU size of 1280 bytes, which after accounting for some of the overhead, leaves a maximum transaction size of 1232 bytes for data in the payload.

With QUIC, we can now use a larger transaction size than the original 1280 bytes. This proposal presents a new transaction size of 4096 bytes.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: P-Token  (the "P" stands for Pinocchio)

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: An optimized rewrite of the SPL Token program for Solana

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content:
Key Highlights
~96% cheaper compute for token instructions.
This frees up 12-13% more block space network-wide (without raising block limits).
Same program ID and interface → fully backward-compatible for wallets, dApps, and users.
Uses a zero-copy architecture built on Pinocchio (Anza's low-level library for efficient Solana programs).
Backed by audits, fuzzing, and formal verification for behavioral parity with the original SPL Token.

Why It Matters
DeFi & high-volume apps: More complex logic per transaction, lower fees during congestion.
Memecoins/airdrop-heavy activity: Massively reduced costs for bulk operations.
Overall network: Better throughput and lower priority fees without users noticing breaking changes — the best kind of upgrade.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content:
I believe Z.ink is already invested in it or in a similar tech because of Star Frame. 

What the Z.ink (SVM) could do is adopt it or fork it with the purpose of lowering overhead and take on more ambitious tasks. 
Overall, I only see positive effects on Z.ink... cheaper, faster token interactions during gameplay which should help blockchain gaming compete against traditional games.
